title: Positioning the Self Scale (PSS-15): Development, Validation, and Psychometric Properties of a Three-Dimensional Measure of Self-Positioning
author: Matta, David (Daoud)
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: The study of the self has long been fragmented across parallel research streams — self-esteem research, social comparison theory, and feedback responsiveness — each generating its own instruments without an integrating framework. The Positioning the Self Scale (PSS-15) addresses this gap by operationalizing a three-dimensional model of self-positioning in which self-worth, openness to feedback, and equality among persons are theorised as interrelated axes of a single relational regulatory system, each lying between opposing distortions of deficiency and excess. This paper reports the development and proposed validation of the PSS-15, a 15-item self-report instrument (5 items per subscale, 5-point Likert format) yielding three independent subscale scores that together generate a self-positioning profile unavailable to single-construct measures. Item generation followed a three-stage procedure comprising theoretical derivation from a 45-item candidate pool, expert review by three independent judges with inter-rater reliability assessed via intraclass correlation (target ICC ≥ .75), and cognitive interviewing with 12 participants using a think-aloud protocol. The proposed validation study targets N = 400 community adults and employs confirmatory factor analysis with MLR estimation to test a hypothesised three-factor oblique structure against four competing models (one-factor, orthogonal, oblique, bifactor), alongside internal consistency (Cronbach's alpha and McDonald's omega, target ≥ .80), four-week test-retest reliability (ICC), convergent and discriminant validity against ten established instruments, and latent profile analysis with 500 random starts to identify theoretically predicted self-positioning configurations. The PSS-15 is designed to distinguish profiles — including narcissism (axes 1 and 3 excess, axis 2 deficit) and imposter syndrome (axis 3 deficit with axis 1 relatively preserved) — that existing instruments cannot differentiate.
